entries.title: The Social Security Reckoning Is Finally Coming
entries.summary: Senators elected this November will be in office when America’s most popular federal program is on track to run out of money.
entries.content:
Senators elected this November will be in office when America’s most popular federal program is on track to run out of money.

Americans have been hearing about the looming demise of Social Security for decades. The number of old people receiving benefits has been increasing much more quickly than the number of working-age adults whose taxes pay for those checks. Yet politicians have let the problem fester for so long that the public could be forgiven for thinking that the crisis would never actually arrive.

But it’s arriving. For the past 16 years, Social Security has paid out more than it has taken in. This has required dipping into its trust fund, built up over the decades when the Baby Boomers were in their peak earning years. That fund is on track to run out by 2032. When it does, benefits will have to be cut by an expected 22 percent across the board to line up with the tax revenue that funds them.

Pretty soon, politicians won’t be able to kick this can down the road anymore. Senators elected this November will be in office in 2032. So will the next president. They will have no choice but to come up with a way to save the federal government’s largest and most popular program, lest they be blamed for its partial collapse. The available solutions are politically tricky but not particularly mysterious. They involve raising taxes, increasing the national debt, and cutting benefits, or some mix of the three. Even if our leaders settle on a way to keep Social Security solvent, however, they are unlikely to address the deeper problem underlying the budgetary one: A program designed to reduce poverty has morphed into a transfer of wealth to the richest members of our society.

In 1935, when the Social Security Act was signed into law, the average person entering the workforce could be expected to die at about 65 years old. Those who lived past that age tended to be too disabled to work, leaving them reliant on their children, if not a local charity or poorhouse. Social Security was designed to ameliorate this problem by levying a tax on workers and their employers, and paying benefits to the elderly roughly in proportion to how much they earned during their career. When the checks first went out, in 1940, the average recipient received $22.60 a month, or about $550 in today’s dollars. Such payments did not require too much sacrifice—just a 1 percent tax on the first $3,000 of a person’s earnings, matched by their employer—because there were many times more workers than there were beneficiaries.

The program ballooned over time. Congress voted repeatedly to expand benefits faster than inflation. But the biggest changes weren’t purposeful. Because benefits are proportional to what a retiree earned during their working years, America’s increasing salaries necessarily made the program grow. So did rising life expectancy. The average 65-year-old now has about 20 years left to live, up from 13 years in 1940. Meanwhile, the pool of workers paying into the system has not kept pace. Fertility rates have fallen, and fewer babies mean fewer workers in the future. The number of workers for each beneficiary has gone down 24 percent since 1990, as more and more is being asked of a smaller share of the population.

The unfortunate fact is that Social Security was never guaranteed to work forever. The tax formula is simple: Each worker, along with their employer, pays 6 percent of their income, up to $184,500. The benefit formula is not. The Social Security Administration takes your highest-earning 35 years of work and calculates your average monthly earnings from those years, adjusted upward for subsequent growth in average wages. A progressive formula determines how much of that you’ll be paid monthly: Assuming that you elect to start receiving benefits at age 67, you’ll get 90 percent of the first $1,300, 32 percent of the next $6,500, and 15 percent of the remaining.

The benefit side of the equation and the tax side of the equation do not add up to the same number. Thanks to the wage adjustment and long life expectancies, most people receive substantially more money than they paid in. In the aggregate, revenues have lagged behind benefits. “There’s no reason those formulas would be compatible with each other,” Andrew Biggs, a former deputy commissioner of the Social Security Administration, told me. The only reason they ever were was that America used to have enough workers to support its population of retirees.

Preventing the program’s insolvency is a math problem that involves tweaking the tax side, the benefit side, or both, until they equal each other. This is not conceptually difficult. Steven Kull, a political psychologist at the University of Maryland, runs a poll every so often in which he shows participants the possible reforms, telling them how much each change closes the funding gap. “The biggest theme is that people solve the problem,” Kull told me, with “majorities taking steps that eliminate most or all of the shortfall.” Respondents tend to be confused as to why their elected leaders haven’t fixed the issue. “The common answer is, That wasn’t that hard. What’s all the fuss about?”

The problem is not that the math is difficult, but that every possible move comes with downsides. “Social Security reform is ultimately about breaking promises,” Biggs said. Either you raise taxes above the rate that people are used to, or you cut benefits for people who were counting on them. Historically, Democrats have preferred the former path, proposing to raise taxes on the well-off to pay for the program’s shortfall while leaving benefits alone. Republicans have traditionally wanted to shrink the program—although typically in a somewhat progressive manner. After President George W. Bush’s unpopular privatization push failed, for example, he proposed a slow flattening of the program by making the benefit formula less generous for all but the bottom 30 percent of recipients.

The Democratic approach has always been more politically popular. Donald Trump was the first Republican to accept that fact. In 2015, as he marched to the Republican nomination, he promised not to touch Social Security, breaking with GOP orthodoxy and thrilling the base. Ever since, the Republican discussion on saving the program has had a distinctly muted tone. A March 2024 white paper by the Republican Study Committee—a caucus that includes most House Republicans—proposed raising the retirement age and “gradually moving towards a flat benefit.” Neither Trump nor Speaker of the House Mike Johnson commented on the proposal. Project 2025, produced by the Heritage Foundation, was more shrewd. The 900-page document contained advice for the future administration on how to properly regulate the shrinking population of greater sage grouse, but it contained no guidance on Social Security.

Democratic plans still revolve around raising taxes while keeping benefits constant or even increasing them. But they now tend to include a feature designed to keep the party’s upper-middle-class base happy: the “doughnut hole.” Many of the current Democratic plans uncap the payroll tax, but typically only for earnings above $400,000, meaning that dollars earned between $185,000 and $400,000 would go untaxed, and everyone who makes less than $185,000 would pay the highest payroll tax rate of all.

Representative John Larson, a Connecticut Democrat, has introduced many Social Security–reform plans over his 14 terms in Congress. “I personally don’t think that there should be a doughnut hole,” he told me. But, he explained, “during the presidential campaign, Biden said that we should lift the cap over $400,000.” Larson came to understand why. “Every town hall I go to, I say, ‘Raise your hand if you’re making more than $400,000,’ and I’ve yet to have a hand go up.” As Democrats have become the party of the pretty-well-off, they have more and more voters who make more than $185,000 but less than $400,000.

The doughnut hole is one weakness, but every plan has its shortcomings. Last month, Senator Elizabeth Warren, a Democrat, and Senator Bernie Moreno, a Republican, announced a proposal to uncap the payroll tax entirely. Their idea gets points for bipartisanship, but it would close just two-thirds of the shortfall, at most, if implemented. Within three years, benefits would again exceed revenues.

A few plans get the math to work out with sufficiently large tax hikes. Proposals by Larson and Senators Bernie Sanders and Sheldon Whitehouse would uncap the payroll tax (with a doughnut hole) and raise taxes on investment income. These are large tax increases: A wealthy self-employed couple making $800,000—imagine married doctors in private practice—would face a marginal federal tax rate of more than 50 percent.

Perhaps the biggest problem with these plans is not the new taxes—it’s what the new taxes pay for. Conceived as an anti-poverty program, Social Security today pays out most of its benefits not to the poor but to the middle class and the rich. The bottom 20 percent of older Americans receive just 7 percent of the total benefits. The economics of old age have changed a lot since the 1930s. The prices of assets—homes and stocks—have soared. As a result of these trends, the elderly are now America’s wealthiest age group, living without children in homes that are on average roomier than everyone else’s. No longer are most of them financially dependent on their children; if anything, their descendants are more likely to ask them for help.

If the public craves a trillion-dollar tax increase on the well-off, there are better ways to spend the money. The federal government already spends about six times as much for each elderly adult as it does each child, even though children and their parents are more than twice as likely to be poor. Uncapping the payroll tax would not close the Social Security shortfall, but it would provide enough money to pay an allowance of $4,500 a child to every family in the country every year. Reforming Social Security by cutting benefits for rich people and raising them for poor people would make the most financial sense and return the program to its roots. President Franklin D. Roosevelt designed Social Security for the elderly not because they were uniquely deserving but because, as he put it in his signing address, they were so “poverty-ridden.” But that scenario—flattening Social Security and focusing future generosity on the young—doesn’t seem particularly likely. Social Security’s popularity comes from its universality, and if well-off people see their benefits reduced, the program could lose some of its widespread appeal. As I reported this story, many wonks recited to me the old political adage, “Programs for the poor make poor programs.”

Indeed, most voters, when faced with the choice, prefer tax increases to benefit cuts. A poll last year by the National Academy of Social Insurance, a network of social-safety-net researchers, showed that the three most popular reform options were slightly different ways to uncap the payroll tax. The next most popular was an increase in benefits. Even raising the payroll-tax rate on everyone by one percentage point ranked above cutting benefits only for the rich. “Broad-based bipartisan majorities say that they’d even be willing to chip in a little bit more themselves,” Rebecca Vallas, the head of NASI, told me, “if that’s what it takes to prevent benefit cuts.”

A social safety net that so heavily favors the old and rich over the needy and young is probably not what most Americans would design if they were starting from scratch, but it’s what we have. The likeliest outcome of whatever Congress comes up with in the next six years is that same dynamic, only more so.

entries.title: The White House’s MAHA Gatekeeper
entries.summary: A policy wonk who could lead the FDA has angered “Make America Healthy Again” activists.
entries.content:
A policy wonk who could lead the FDA has angered “Make America Healthy Again” activists.

The world’s most popular podcaster, Joe Rogan, reached out privately in April to the world’s most powerful man, Donald Trump, to discuss the healing power of ibogaine, a psychedelic made from the root bark of a Central African shrub that researchers are studying as a treatment for addiction and trauma. Trump responded by text, “‘Sounds great. Do you want FDA approval? Let’s do it,’” Rogan announced weeks later, when he stood behind the president in the Oval Office for the signing of an executive order on psychedelic therapies. “It was literally that quick.”

But it wasn’t that simple. After hearing from Rogan, the president told his team at the Department of Health and Human Services to change the policy, and the team sent a draft executive order to the White House Domestic Policy Council. That’s when things got messy. In the days that followed, Rogan’s allies engaged in battle with a mid-level White House health adviser, Dr. Heidi Overton. As a deputy assistant to the president who focuses on health policy, Overton ran a process to revise the draft in coordination with other stakeholders, including the Departments of Justice and Veterans Affairs.

Members of the “Make America Healthy Again” movement were furious, convinced that Overton, not for the first time, had undermined Trump’s own desires. The friction, which has not been previously reported, became so intense that Rogan and W. Bryan Hubbard, the CEO of Americans for Ibogaine, a nonprofit that champions the drug’s therapeutic benefits, threatened to pull out of the signing ceremony if they did not have a say in Overton’s final edits, according to three people familiar with the events.

Overton’s efforts on the Domestic Policy Council shrank the roughly 1,400-word HHS draft to about 900 words by the time Trump signed his name. Entire sections were removed, including those calling for a psychedelic-therapy czar and treatment-funding reforms, according to a draft we reviewed. A $250 million research fund became a $50 million partnership with states. In the end, the document accelerated review for some drugs and pushed the government to reschedule psychedelic substances that perform well in large clinical trials, enough to persuade Rogan and Hubbard to show for the signing.

Overton is one of the most powerful public-health officials in Trump’s orbit who has little public profile. Her job, as a senior health-policy official on the White House Domestic Policy Council, is to convert the president’s priorities into the scope and language of documents that will advance the White House’s agenda. But Overton’s detractors describe her as one of the biggest obstacles to MAHA actually realizing its priorities—“the controlled opposition,” one person who has worked with her told us. They accuse her of slow-walking marijuana rescheduling, trying to limit MAHA changes to federal nutrition guidelines, and delaying or weakening several other initiatives. “As an unelected bureaucrat, Heidi Overton sought to use her pen to override the intended exercise of presidential power,” one person outside of government who was familiar with the psychedelic-executive-order process told us. “It was a derogation of representative democracy.”

Leaders of the West Wing and HHS, by contrast, say the 37-year-old physician represents democracy at work. They have rushed to her defense, describing her as a star scientific wonk with a medical degree and a doctorate in clinical investigation who knows how to balance competing interests to achieve Trump’s goals. “The president adores her,” White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told us. Since the start of Trump’s second term, the White House has trusted Overton to coordinate nearly all of its major health policies, including MAHA priorities such as revisions to childhood-vaccine recommendations and the redesign of the food pyramid.

“In President Trump’s administration, the Domestic Policy Council is the White House office that is and should be depended on to make sure things are correct, legally durable, done with policy consistency, and, in this case, medically sound,” White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles told us. “Any suggestion that Heidi Overton or the Domestic Policy Council is slowing things down is being made by an uninformed person. Things move at a light speed in this administration.” The White House has been sufficiently impressed by Overton’s work that she has been named as one of three finalists to become the next Food and Drug Administration commissioner, a promotion that would give her direct power over the pharmaceutical industry, medical devices, tobacco products, and most of the nation’s food.

The fight over Overton’s role in the first 18 months of Trump’s second term is certain to become more public if she faces Senate confirmation later this year. It has already revealed a growing rift between the White House and a group of MAHA policy makers over the pace and limits of reforming federal health policy.

Sky-high expectations for radical change at the start of the administration yielded some dramatic shifts on vaccine recommendations, nutrition guidelines, pharmaceutical regulation, and agricultural policy. But the team that Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. built has faced pushback on pesticide regulation and other areas where they sought to disrupt decades of federal policy making. Overton, as the traffic cop of health policy at the White House, has found herself at the center of those fights. This story is based on interviews with 17 people with direct knowledge of Overton’s work. Nearly all of them requested anonymity because they are not authorized to speak publicly or feared retribution from the White House if they publicly criticized her. Overton declined to comment for this story.

Kennedy, the founder and public face of the Make America Healthy Again movement, who has from the start of the administration found himself limited by the interagency process that Overton runs, is nevertheless one of her most vocal defenders. At the signing ceremony for the psychedelics executive order, he thanked Overton while acknowledging the behind-closed- doors fights that had taken place. “It was often frustrating but always right,” he said of her advice. “She brings exceptional dedication, meticulous attention to detail, and strategic leadership to every policy she touches, turning our vision into meaningful results for the American people,” Kennedy told us in a statement.

But supporters of Kennedy’s MAHA ambitions say that he has been frustrated at times with the limits that Overton and the White House have put on him. “Time after time her views were to dig in and resist the president’s agenda until his friends called him directly and overrode her,” another person familiar with her work told us, pointing to issues such as psychedelic treatments for veterans and marijuana rescheduling.

At the HHS headquarters building last year, a joke began circulating about all of the policies that Kennedy advisers believed Overton was holding up, according to three sources: Policy proposals that go to the White House fall into what they call the “Overton window” of inaction—a play on the social-science term named after an unrelated researcher that describes the range of acceptable change in a political system.

Overton began working for Trump in 2019 while completing a surgical residency at Johns Hopkins University, where she became an ally of the last FDA commissioner, Marty Makary. She joined the White House at a time of crisis, serving on the front lines of the nation’s COVID response. Allies say that the experience left her skeptical of the established public-health infrastructure, pushing her to finish her doctorate in clinical investigation at Hopkins and perhaps making her receptive to the reforms called for by the MAHA movement. After Trump’s defeat in 2020, she joined Brooke Rollins, her former boss at the White House Domestic Policy Council, at America First Policy Institute, a think tank that doubled as a Cabinet-in-waiting for a second Trump term.

Rollins became agriculture secretary when Trump returned to the White House. Overton helped Kennedy put together his team for HHS, people involved told us. She supported Trump’s embrace of the MAHA agenda, people who interacted with her at the time told us. Top Trump advisers trusted her to support the cause while running the scientific traps, keeping an eye on the political risks, and managing the contentious inter-department fights that Kennedy’s proposals would inevitably create.

One of Kennedy’s first major MAHA drives was to replace what he called the “unreadable” scientific groundwork by the Biden administration to revise federal nutrition guidelines. He launched a new push for “gold standard science,” prompting a monthslong rush to develop new evidence reviews to back up the guidelines that Kennedy wanted to issue. Those effectively flipped the 1990s USDA “food pyramid” to prioritize the consumption of proteins, healthy fats, and fibrous vegetables—what Kennedy considers real food—over refined grains and ultra-processed foods. Rollins, at USDA, and Kennedy, at HHS, made a show of working closely together. But behind the scenes, their departments engaged in sometimes acrimonious battle, and Overton was in the center, mediating the back-and-forth.

HHS officials became convinced that Overton was siding with USDA. Wary of a legal deadline to produce the new recommendations, she chafed at Kennedy’s idea of throwing out all of the Biden administration’s work. She wanted to keep the parts that the Trump administration had no qualms about, such as research done on saturated fats. And some HHS officials viewed her as sympathetic to USDA concerns about a lack of consultation with the new scientific advisory panel that HHS had appointed, two people said.

“At one point, her recommendation was Can we just use the old science?” one person involved in the process told us. “Long story short, she did everything she could to prevent the real-food thing from being a success.” HHS officials became so concerned that Overton was favoring USDA and undermining their efforts that they created a binder, documenting every communication among her and USDA officials in a bid to better counter her arguments, according to two people familiar with the effort.

Overton’s allies say that to suggest that she did not want a major remake of nutrition guidelines is just wrong. They point to the end result as evidence. Overton also subsequently led the policy process that resulted in USDA committing $700 million to a regenerative-farming program, championed by Kennedy, that seeks to avoid heavy dependence on chemical fertilizer and pesticides. She also helped write the first MAHA executive order last February, which reframed the government’s health priorities around countering chronic diseases, reversing declining life expectancy, and reducing supposed overreliance on medication.

But other changes came more slowly, to the frustration of MAHA advocates. Recently, Overton has been involved in drafting new policies for the use of peptides, short chains of amino acids that are available on the gray market but that, in many cases, have scant scientific evidence to support their safety or efficacy. An FDA advisory board is meeting this week to consider whether to recommend easing regulations on seven popular peptides, allowing them to be made by compounding pharmacies, which prepare custom medications that in many cases aren’t available from traditional pharmacies.

MAHA advocates support greater peptide availability, often portraying them as preferable to conventional pharmaceuticals. Kennedy himself has acknowledged using peptides. Overton, however, is viewed by some as wary of the lack of scientific research into some of the compounds. For her supporters, this is proof that she is doing her job. For her opponents, it is evidence that she is a bureaucratic institutionalist representing the kind of inaction that MAHA had hoped to break. “Heidi is a skeptic of anything that is not pharma, and that would include peptides,” a person familiar with the matter told us.

Overton was also in the middle of a fierce internal debate over marijuana regulation. Trump made a 2024 campaign promise to reclassify the drug from Schedule I (a criminal designation that highlights abuse potential for substances without medical value) to Schedule III (lower abuse risk and an allowance for medical prescription and study.) Law-enforcement agencies and conservatives were wary of marijuana’s harms, and the Trump administration stalled on the initiative for almost all of last year.

Marijuana entrepreneurs and one of the president’s wealthy friends, Howard Kessler, eventually triumphed with direct appeals to the president, creating a playbook that Rogan followed in his outreach on loosening the regulation of psychedelics. Those direct appeals were designed, to some degree, to bypass the interagency process, people familiar with the matter told us. Once Trump decided to move, he relied on Overton to make the change happen. “I’ve never been inundated by so many people as I have about this particular reclassification,” Trump said at a December signing of an executive order that finally directed the marijuana-classification change. Kessler developed a direct relationship with Overton during the process and has since been working with her on other health issues.

In late December, Trump pushed for another MAHA health initiative to be codified in short order, calling for a new childhood-vaccination schedule to be in place by the end of the year. Once again, Overton was in the middle of wrangling between the White House and HHS. She and department officials created an initial draft, but HHS lawyers initially rejected the proposal as lacking enough scientific backing and therefore vulnerable to legal challenge. At a meeting that month in the Eisenhower Executive Office Building, next to the White House, Overton dressed down HHS officials for not presenting a document that would pass legal review, according to a person who attended. “I think she felt blindsided,” the person said.

HHS staff worked through Christmas and New Year’s to footnote the document, recruiting Martin Kulldorff, a former Harvard biostatistician who now works in Kennedy’s office, to help. The new rules were introduced in January. A federal judge blocked them in March, ruling that the agency had not done a proper scientific review even after the later revisions.

Kennedy suggested last year that Overton take the job overseeing the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in Atlanta, an offer that she declined, according to two people familiar with the situation. But in recent weeks, Overton has made clear that she is open to running the FDA, a larger and more powerful agency. White House officials are also considering two others: Jeff Vacirca, a cancer physician serving as the CEO and chairman of the board of New York Cancer & Blood Specialists, and the senior Pentagon health official Stephen Ferrara. A decision is expected soon.

Trump’s psychedelic order came together in weeks. Shortly after Rogan reached out to Trump, they saw each other at an Ultimate Fighting Championship event in Miami. “He came to me and said, It’s done. We’re going to take care of it. It’s a good thing for the soldiers. It’s a good thing for everybody,” Rogan later recounted on his podcast.

Some in the White House were caught off guard and questioned the wisdom of unleashing psychedelic mental-health treatments that had not yet been studied like other approved pharmaceuticals. Officials at the Department of Veterans Affairs were concerned about patients suddenly demanding treatments that the department was not ready to provide. Overton once again stepped in. “She wanted to make sure it was being done the right way,” Chris Klomp, a senior adviser to Kennedy, told us. “What I observed with the psychedelics is that Heidi ran a process to try to align all these different pieces.”

But Klomp said that he was not surprised that the process ended with some people unhappy. “The Domestic Policy Council is in no small part a quarterback aligning all those different parties,” Klomp said. “If you don’t understand that process, you can at times feel pretty frustrated.”

MAHA activists, who view Trump’s presidency and Kennedy as a rare opportunity for major change, see Overton’s more cautious, deliberate approach as anathema. Overton’s allies say that this is a misreading of her role, which is to build consensus and deliver a lasting outcome. Now, as Trump considers moving her to a much larger position, the debate may soon find a wider audience.

entries.title: No War for Old Men
entries.summary: The fall of Ukraine’s top general, Oleksandr Syrsky, leaves younger officers to finish the reforms that he resisted.
entries.content:
The fall of Ukraine’s top general, Oleksandr Syrsky, leaves younger officers to finish the reforms that he resisted.

Drone warfare took a while to catch on in Ukraine. Like any revolution, it required a struggle. The opposition came mostly from the older generals trained to fight using artillery and tanks. Early in the war with Russia, many of them saw drones as toys, better suited to taking aerial photos of wedding parties than flying missions over the battlefield. But a coalition of junior officers and tech enthusiasts saw early on that drones could hold the key to Ukraine’s defense, even its victory.

The course of the war has proved the younger generation right. Drones now account for more than 80 percent of all battlefield casualties. They have made tanks and artillery look like lumbering relics in the war zone. Through long-range strikes against Russian oil refineries and other infrastructure, drones have also forced Moscow to play defense. But it took years for the technology to gain broad acceptance.

“We had no financing at first,” Mykhailo Fedorov, the main proponent of drone warfare in Ukraine, told me in an interview last year. The first attack-drone units formed in Ukraine in 2023, about a year into the full-scale war with Russia. “We were going around, asking for money from businesspeople, fundraising on Facebook,” he said.

At the time, Fedorov served as the minister of digital transformation, a position that gave him no power to buy weapons on the state’s behalf. Through persistence, showmanship, and a knack for technocratic knife-fighting, he circumvented the rules of military procurement, turning his ministry into a hub for Ukraine’s nascent drone program. “The revolution happened inside the bureaucracy,” he told me.

So did the counterrevolution. Its leaders sat a couple of miles away, behind the neoclassical facade of the General Staff headquarters. Oleksandr Syrsky, the commander of Ukraine’s armed forces, was chief among them. He trained in the Soviet school of military theory and often clashed with Fedorov over the role that drones should play in the Ukrainian arsenal. Their conflict intensified at the start of this year, when Fedorov took over the Ministry of Defense, a post that finally gave him control over the purchase of weapons.

Over the past six months, Fedorov and Syrsky frequently took their complaints about each other to the arbiter in their disagreements, President Volodymyr Zelensky. This month, their constant infighting became too much for the president, and he decided to fire first Fedorov, and then Syrsky. But he did not resolve the questions at the center of their conflict. Should Ukraine, in the middle of a war for its survival, continue to restructure the machinery of its defense in ways that no military power has ever attempted? Or would that strategy end up weakening the forces tasked with holding the line against Russia?

Ukraine’s war effort now faces a leadership vacuum, and neither camp has a clear mandate to complete Ukraine’s history-making experiment in warfare or to reverse it. The drone revolution, having already reached a point of no return, has now lost its greatest advocate in the top ranks of the government. The result could be a level of indecision that the military cannot afford.

On Monday, the day before he was fired, General Syrsky issued a call for the tech accelerationists inside the government to pump the brakes. Drones alone cannot defeat the Russian army, and the culture of Silicon Valley cannot supplant the need for military discipline, Syrsky argued in a column for Militarnyi, a Ukrainian publication focused on defense. “It is an obvious gamble,” he wrote, addressing the push for Ukraine to outsource the work of soldiers to artificially intelligent machines. “Any experiment of this scale is paid for by our lands and our people.”

Some parts of the piece reminded me of an old man shaking his fist at a passing drone, frustrated that the world he knows is moving on. But Syrsky, who is 60 years old, made a lot of cogent points that I have often heard from senior officers. In any war, the top generals need to synchronize the forces under their command to make them function as a single organism, with weapons and tactics that work in concert with one another. The nature of the war in Ukraine has made that challenge particularly daunting.

The front line spans more than 600 miles, and its Ukrainian defenders include a dizzying array of units from various branches of the military. Early in the war, many of them got into the habit of raising money to purchase weapons and gear for themselves. Crowdfunding campaigns on social media brought in enormous sums from around the world, and much of it went toward the purchase of drones on the open market.

The practice spawned a parallel system of procurement in the military, whereby low-level commanders routinely placed orders from the suppliers of their choice. Fedorov championed this practice. From his post in the ministry of digital transformation, he launched an online bazaar akin to Amazon that sells drones and other high-tech weapons to the military. Soldiers of any rank can scroll through thousands of gadgets on the Brave1 marketplace, including wheeled robots, remote-controlled bomber drones, and microchips to upgrade them with AI-powered targeting and other capabilities.

The market has no precedent in modern warfare. By law, the armed forces of Ukraine, like every major military in the world, centralizes weapons orders via the defense ministry. Fedorov saw that system as far too cumbersome and prone to corruption. It could not deliver weapons fast enough, he argued, and it did not cater to the ever-changing requirements of soldiers at the front. In January, when Zelensky promoted him to defense minister, Fedorov tried to supplant the traditional procurement system with a version of the marketplace.

Syrsky saw that as a threat to his authority, and it’s hard to blame him. When junior officers can decide how to equip their men, keeping everything in sync across the battlefield becomes more difficult. The lower ranks might appreciate the freedom and adaptability of this arrangement, but their commanding officers in Kyiv do not. “The General Staff provides the ministry with a complete list of what is needed for war,” Syrsky wrote in his Militarnyi column, listing artillery, mortars and “dozens of other items.” He added: “Everything must be purchased, not just what is currently in vogue.”

By the time Fedorov lost his job last week, his years of advocacy for high-tech warfare had been vindicated. Ukraine now produces several million drones a year, easing its dependence on foreign military aid. Most of the credit for that belongs to the intricate clockwork of drone factories, start-ups, software developers, training academies, and military innovators that help keep the Ukrainian war machine running. Fedorov, who is 35 years old, has long been the face of this ecosystem and its chief advocate inside the halls of power. It should therefore not have surprised Zelensky that firing Fedorov caused a fierce public backlash. Thousands of demonstrators gathered in Kyiv and other cities across Ukraine to demand his reinstatement. Many of the protesters called for Syrsky to be ousted instead.

The president obliged by firing the general, but he did not bring back the minister of defense. As a consolation, he offered Fedorov a position on the National Security and Defense Council, an advisory body that usually acts as a retirement home for senior officials and military brass. Fedorov declined, in part because the council has no authority to purchase weapons. Nor can it enact the ambitious reforms Fedorov left unfinished.

In place of these two seasoned members of Zelensky’s team, the president installed a pair of lesser-known figures. Although both are experienced and well regarded in the military, they lack the institutional chops and perhaps also the inclination to carry on the bureaucratic battles that felled their predecessors.

The most obvious quality they have in common might be their youth. The new acting minister of defense, Yevhenii Khmara, and the new commander of the armed forces, Mykhailo Drapatyi, both belong to the same generation as Fedorov and Zelensky. They never studied in the Soviet academies where Syrsky learned the military doctrines he has relied on throughout this war.

But that does not mean the younger generation has won a mandate to continue Fedorov’s more radical agenda. Zelensky has not granted it. His new appointees will likely face the same resistance from the older cadres in the general staff. Without Fedorov in charge of the ministry of defense, Ukraine’s revolution in warfare will lose its standard-bearer and a lot of its momentum—just when those innovations appeared to be paying off.

entries.title: A Planeload of Informants
entries.summary: The United States is intensifying its hunt for narcos within the Mexican government. Is this all prelude to an attack?
entries.content:
The United States is intensifying its hunt for narcos within the Mexican government. Is this all prelude to an attack?

The governor of Baja California is a 40-year-old attorney and former big-city mayor named Marina del Pilar. Her state, which includes the megacity of Tijuana, is a magnet for American tourists, and also for narcotics cartels. As a politician of some experience, she is presumably unfazed by the sorts of challenges that come with governing a volatile section of her volatile country.

And yet, in what has quickly become a very famous recording of a phone call that leaked last week, she has difficulty masking her shock about an unusual predicament. “Are they saying they want to extradite me?” she asks her interlocutor, who is apparently an intermediary for the FBI. The “they” in question is the United States government. Almost immediately, her tone becomes more businesslike, and she makes a not-so-cryptic offer: “Can’t they tell me what they want me to talk about? I might know or hear a lot of security-related things.”

In other words, the governor of the strategically important border state of Baja California was just caught on tape offering to become an FBI informant. The remarkable thing here is that she would not be the first; the list of current and former Mexican officials and drug traffickers who have offered to cooperate with the U.S. government is now lengthy.

The phone exchange, released by the Mexican journalist Héctor de Mauleón, seemed almost too Narcos to be real, until del Pilar confirmed its authenticity. Her predecessor had arranged the phone call, she said, after the U.S. revoked her visa last year. As more recordings leaked in recent days, the country was transfixed. Columnists theorized about what del Pilar might know that she would be willing to trade for more favorable treatment. The Americans seemed to want her testimony for a bigger case—but who was their target?

In order to fully grasp why politicians like del Pilar are considering cooperating with U.S. investigators, it is vital to keep in mind the current, overwhelming Mexican reality, which is that the country’s leaders believe that President Trump will intensify America’s intervention in Mexico. Their gravest concern—a plausible one—is that Trump will launch military strikes against the country.

Since his second term began, Trump has periodically threatened military action inside Mexico, alluding to air strikes or operations that would “take out the cartels.” Mexican officials have repeatedly rejected the possibility. “There will be no invasion of Mexico,” President Claudia Sheinbaum said last year. But quietly, Mexican officials have discussed what it would mean if they were attacked by their neighbor and largest trading partner. Would it be a special-forces operation, like the one that seized former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro? A drone strike on a fentanyl lab? Some Mexican officials are reading books about the Mexican-American War, searching for historical precedent.

Because the threat of military intervention is so dramatic, not enough attention has been paid to a type of intervention that is already taking place, one that could pave the way for strikes. In a break with long-standing precedent, American prosecutors have used informants to pursue members of the Mexican government, apparently “moving up the food chain,” as one U.S. official described it to me. Del Pilar has become the most famous example of such cooperation. The State Department canceled her U.S. visa last year while law-enforcement agencies investigated her and her then-husband. In the leaked audio recordings, the supposed FBI representatives refer to “sanctions” and “charges” against her, though no charges have been filed. Del Pilar makes clear that she is willing to offer information gleaned from state security meetings in order to avoid being extradited. She says vaguely that what she hears in those meetings “isn’t what I hear elsewhere,” possibly referring to sensitive information that might help investigators. On Sunday, del Pilar released a video saying she was merely trying to find ways to reinstate her canceled visa. I messaged her spokesperson to ask about this, but she didn’t respond. The FBI has also declined to comment.

Several former Mexican officials facing U.S. charges have turned themselves in, apparently agreeing to cooperate with authorities to reduce their sentences. Dozens of newly extradited drug traffickers, too, are believed to be cooperating. Their accounts appear to have allowed U.S. investigators to build cases against members of Sheinbaum’s Morena political party. At least four have been indicted. Many more have had their visas revoked.

It’s a high-risk escalation in the Trump administration’s fight against narco corruption—and possibly also a pretext for a unilateral attack. Trump could use evidence of the government’s links to organized crime in an attempt to justify using force in Mexico. This week, the Trump-appointed head of the Drug Enforcement Administration, Terry Cole, gave a speech describing “the deadly connection between cartel networks and the Mexican government.”

“They are one in the same,” he said.

The most senior member of Sheinbaum’s party to face charges is Ruben Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa. He was indicted in April for alleged drug trafficking. Prosecutors have accused him of supporting a faction of the Sinaloa cartel, which in turn used kidnappings, intimidation, and ballot theft to help Morena candidates win elections. Sheinbaum has defended him, claiming that the indictment lacks evidence.

“An action of that magnitude has no precedent in the history of our bilateral relationship,” Sheinbaum said at a political rally in May, referring to the DOJ’s request for the arrest, for extradition purposes, of 10 Mexican citizens. “Is this a genuine, legitimate interest in helping Mexico? Is it a genuine commitment to combating organized crime? Or are we witnessing segments of the U.S. far right using our country to position themselves ahead of their 2026 elections?”

Ironically, it was Sheinbaum’s cooperation with the United States that helped bolster the American caseload. As Trump threatened to invade Mexico, her administration last year quietly increased its security ties to Washington, an apparent peace offering aimed at deterring an attack as well as an effort by Sheinbaum to rein in organized crime. The Mexican government extradited or transferred more than 100 alleged criminals to the United States from February 2025 to January 2026—many times the previous flow. Then, in February, the Mexican military killed Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, known as “El Mencho,” partially based on U.S. intelligence.

Many of the newly extradited Mexicans—facing the possibility of life in prison—have become cooperating witnesses in exchange for the possibility of reduced sentences, according to current and former U.S. officials. El Mencho, meanwhile, left behind ledgers documenting bribes and payments, according to one U.S. official, which could be used as evidence. And now del Pilar and other Mexican officials, threatened with yet another round of extraditions and canceled U.S. visas, are apparently talking to U.S. law enforcement. If those cases advance, and Mexican officials offer damning information about their colleagues, it could lead to another cascade of indictments.

“That’s the challenge for Sheinbaum. Once you uncork the bottle, there’s no controlling what comes out,” Robert Patterson, the former acting administrator of the DEA, told me. “And a credible investigation doesn’t pick and choose who it touches or who it holds to account.”

For years, even as the United States pursued Mexico’s most famous drug kingpins, investigators observed an unofficial red line: The Justice Department did not indict senior sitting officials or politicians. Those cases could shatter diplomatic relations. U.S. officials complained privately about pervasive corruption in Mexico, but they never attempted to extradite the worst offenders while they were still in office.

Even when prosecutors charged former Mexican officials, the response could be explosive. Months after arresting former Defense Minister Salvador Cienfuegos in 2020 on drug-trafficking charges during a visit to Los Angeles, the United States bowed to political pressure and returned him to Mexico. He continues to live as a free man. It was a lesson in the way U.S. prosecutorial goals could be flattened by Mexican politics.

Yet Rocha’s indictment and the pressure on del Pilar suggest a radical change. The United States is now willing to pursue members of the ruling party, in spite of the rift it might cause. It’s a strategy that has confused many current and former U.S. officials, particularly given the unlikelihood that Rocha will ever be extradited. He has temporarily resigned from his post but has denied the charges against him. “I’m scratching my head on what the goal is here,” said one former narcotics prosecutor.

The most frightening scenario to many officials on both sides of the border is that the United States might use Rocha’s case as a pretext to strike Mexico. They point to what happened after Maduro was indicted on drug charges. Mexico, of course, is not Venezuela. It is the United States’ biggest trading partner, the largest democracy in the Spanish-speaking world, home to some 1.5 million U.S. citizens, and visited by approximately 40 million every year. The fallout from any unilateral U.S. action would hit both sides of the border.

And yet, for the first time in more than a century, the United States is not backing away. Last week, the State Department designated two lesser-known drug cartels—the Juárez Cartel and Los Viagras—as “foreign terrorist ⁠organizations” and “specially designated global terrorists.” Some within the Trump administration believe that those designations would offer some kind of legal authority for an attack, though international-law experts largely differ.

As the U.S. has shifted its legal strategy in Mexico, the cartel landscape, too, has changed dramatically. Nearly all of the drug kingpins of the past two decades are in prison or dead. Most famously, El Chapo is sitting in a maximum-security prison in Colorado. El Mencho was killed by the Mexican military. Ismael Zambada García, “El Mayo,” was—bewilderingly—delivered to U.S. authorities on a New Mexico airstrip in 2024 by one of El Chapo’s sons, Joaquín Guzmán López, who apparently hoped to earn favorable treatment from U.S. prosecutors. Another son, Ovidio Guzmán López, is also cooperating after being extradited in 2023.

The campaign against cartel leaders has been so successful that, even if the United States wanted to decapitate a drug-trafficking organization, it’s unclear whom they would target. “For an administration that thrives on publicity, if the goal is to make a big splash by taking out someone who is a big household name, that well is running very dry,” Adam Isaacson, the director of the defense-oversight program at the Washington Office on Latin America, told me.

Some experts had been concerned that the absence of kingpins could lead to a power vacuum and a spike in violence. That hasn’t happened. Homicides in Mexico fell by nearly half from September 2024 to May 2026.

With the pressure on its government rising, Mexico has begun to make its own demands of the United States. Last week, Sheinbaum said that she would request criminal charges in the cases of Mexican citizens killed by ICE. The week before, she demanded clarity about the U.S. role in the surrender of Joaquín Guzmán López in New Mexico. And her administration reminded the Americans of another grievance: Since January 1, 2018, Mexico has requested the extradition of 269 alleged criminals whose cases have yet to be approved by the United States.

After years of deriding Mexican inaction on security, the United States appears to have found a willing partner. Now it risks forfeiting that cooperation. On Monday, as if to demonstrate what could be lost, Mexican authorities arrested Janero Van DeMario Garrett, allegedly a member of the Sinaloa cartel. “The arrest was made possible thanks to mechanisms of international cooperation and the exchange of information with the DEA,” Mexico’s security minister wrote on X.

The alleged criminal, it turned out, was a U.S. citizen.

entries.title: The Art of the Giveaway
entries.summary: The Trump administration is giving Saudi Arabia exactly what it wants. For what?
entries.content:
The Trump administration is giving Saudi Arabia exactly what it wants. For what?

What kind of negotiator gives the other side everything that it wants with the hope of eventually getting something in return?

It’s a question that even some of President Trump’s own allies are asking after his administration signed a landmark deal to develop a civilian nuclear program in Saudi Arabia using American technology. The deal’s opponents, in the United States and Israel, worry that the new pact might ultimately allow for uranium enrichment—and if so, that Trump just gave Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman the tools he needs to build a bomb. What did the U.S. require in return? Pretty much zip.

That, at least, is how things stood when the agreement was finalized yesterday. Then, just before 8 a.m. today, Trump took to social media to deliver what seemed like an after-the-fact ultimatum: The deal, he wrote, is “totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords,” the Middle East initiative launched in Trump’s first term that is designed to normalize relations between Israel and countries in the region.

Trump-administration officials argued that the deal is an “America First” policy triumph because the 30-year, multibillion-dollar agreement gives American companies (which already do huge business in Saudi Arabia) a central role in developing the kingdom’s nuclear infrastructure while shutting out foreign competitors. Karoline Leavitt, the White House press secretary, told reporters today that the arrangement “will ultimately benefit American industry workers and supply chains.”

It may not benefit Trump’s image as a consummate dealmaker, however. The framework of Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham accords and, in return, getting defense guarantees and U.S. help to develop a civilian nuclear program has been on the table for years. It came close to fruition during the Biden administration. But the kingdom received a defense package in November and the nuclear deal this week. Neither agreement stipulated the normalizing of relations with Israel as a precondition. “Leave it to this administration to negotiate after the signatures are barely dry,” Elisa Ewers, a senior fellow for Middle East studies at the Council on Foreign Relations, told us.

When the agreement was unveiled yesterday, U.S. lawmakers and Israeli politicians were startled by its lax conditions for Riyadh. Trump by this morning may have decided to provide reassurance with his Abraham Accords announcement—so he did what he often does.

“The president wakes up every day, and every day, at least on Middle East policy, is an improvisation,” F. Gregory Gause, a professor emeritus at the Bush School of Government and Public Service at Texas A&M University, told us. “He was getting some pushback on the Saudi deal, and so he added this sweetener to it for people in Congress who might feel that if the Saudis had a regular diplomatic relationship with Israel, they would be less concerned.”

Trump, he noted, may improvise again and remove the stipulation if the furor dies down. Last week, the president said that the U.S. would impose a 20 percent fee on the value of the cargo on ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz to counter Iranian efforts to control the waterway. The next day, the president reversed himself. “So it could be that by tomorrow, this goes away from the president’s mind,” Gause said. But if he gives the Saudis what they want without asking for anything in return, he also would have thrown away all of his leverage.

In 2023, top officials in the Biden administration shuttled between Washington and Riyadh, believing that they were on the precipice of a major breakthrough: Bin Salman, known as MBS, was close to joining the Abraham Accords, a development that officials saw as a bulwark against Iran’s rising nuclear threat in the region. Talks to flesh out the details were scheduled for mid-October of that year. But the negotiations unraveled after Hamas’s October 7 attack on Israel and the devastating Gaza war that followed. In the aftermath, Saudi leaders concluded that normalization was politically untenable.

Biden-administration officials then looked for other ways to entice the Saudis to both restart normalization talks and help secure the Gaza Strip. They focused on a mutual-defense agreement, which included nuclear cooperation, even though President Biden had made known his dislike for the crown prince after the 2018 murder of the journalist Jamal Khashoggi. Jon Wolfsthal, a former adviser on U.S.-Saudi nuclear cooperation, told us that the Biden administration had calculated that “if the U.S. didn’t get in the game, particularly as a nuclear provider, one of America’s adversaries might, and that is much worse for U.S. national security.”

But with the 2024 election looming, Saudi Arabia’s government gambled that a Trump victory would bring more favorable terms, and the talks stalled.

The Saudis were right. Saudi leaders already saw in Trump a partner whose approach was similar to their own: pragmatic, transactional, and largely unbothered by concerns about human rights. The relationship extended beyond official diplomacy. MBS had developed a close rapport with Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner during Trump’s first term, communicating with him directly over WhatsApp. Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund had previously committed $2 billion to Kushner’s private-equity firm.

Following his return to the Oval Office, Trump grew fixated on getting Saudi Arabia to normalize ties with Israel. As in his first term, Trump picked Saudi Arabia for his first official overseas visit, arriving with an entourage of American business executives in an effort to foster deeper corporate ties, particularly in technology. During a meeting in Washington, D.C., in November, Trump, raising his voice, pressed MBS to join the Abraham Accords. MBS insisted that the only way to achieve this would be by agreeing on a path toward Palestinian statehood. (Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has rejected the prospect of a sovereign Palestinian state.) Still, the U.S. and Saudi Arabia reached an understanding on a defense agreement, which would include the sale of advanced F-35 fighter jets.

Months later, Trump tried to link an expansion of the Abraham Accords to his plan for peace with Iran, suggesting in a conference call with several regional leaders that they sign up, according to U.S. and Middle Eastern officials. After that conversation finished, he called MBS personally to press the issue once more. But Trump got the same answer. Still, talks over a Saudi civilian nuclear program continued.

The Trump administration says that this week’s deal is designed to help Saudi Arabia develop civilian nuclear power only. Energy Secretary Chris Wright, who signed the agreement, said in a statement that the terms “uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation.” The Saudi government said that the deal supports its domestic efforts to diversify energy sources, and that the government is committed to the “highest international standards” of nuclear safety, security, and nonproliferation.

But Saudi Arabia has also talked about becoming a nuclear-weapons power if Iran were able to develop its own weapon. Preventing Tehran from doing so is a central goal of the U.S. conflict, but its outcome is uncertain, as is the fate of Iran’s nuclear program. “Saudi Arabia does not want to acquire any nuclear bomb, but without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible,” MBS told CBS News in 2018.

Advocates of nonproliferation contend that the U.S.-Saudi deal lacks the necessary guardrails to prevent that: no measures to prevent the sharing of uranium-enrichment and plutonium-separation technologies, no commitment by Saudi Arabia to comply with the safety standards of, and monitoring by, the International Atomic Energy Agency.

Under the agreement, American companies would build nuclear reactors in Saudi Arabia, and the two countries would study whether Saudi Arabia should eventually enrich uranium on its own instead of importing nuclear fuel. Enriching uranium is necessary to produce the fuel for nuclear-power plants but can also be a key step toward making a nuclear weapon. “Your best route to avoiding proliferation is this kind of very, very close relationship with the United States, with U.S. technology, that we control, that we monitor, and that is of a long duration,” Victoria Coates, a former deputy national security adviser to Trump, who now works at the Heritage Foundation, told us.

Trump denied that enrichment is on the table. The deal notes that the U.S. will ultimately decide, after a study is conducted, whether enrichment shouldn’t take place, and if that’s the conclusion, Saudi Arabia would be barred from pursuing it on its own or with another country for 10 years. But giving the U.S., rather than international inspectors, that authority could lead other regimes to choose their own inspectors too, opening the door to broader proliferation.

“Making all of this subjective—and ‘you can choose your own inspectors,’ and all those other things—is a really bad route to go down,” Jon Alterman, a global-security scholar at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, told us. In theory, he said, the agreement could give Iranians the license and precedent to credibly say: “We have Russian inspectors, we have Chinese inspectors, so we’re doing just what the Saudis are doing.”

Alterman noted that the U.S. even has a model for a better arrangement: a 2009 agreement with the United Arab Emirates. “The Emiratis built a genuinely robust and safe domestic nuclear program,” Alterman said. “There are robust safeguards against proliferation. And it deepened the partnership between the UAE and the United States.” But that’s not what the U.S. asked of Saudi Arabia this week.

entries.title: North Carolina’s Threat to Legal Assistance for the Poor
entries.summary: Republicans in the state legislature abruptly slashed funding for civil legal aid for low-income clients this month.
entries.content:
Republicans in the state legislature abruptly slashed funding for civil legal aid for low-income clients this month.

This is an edition of The Atlantic Daily, a newsletter that guides you through the biggest stories of the day, helps you discover new ideas, and recommends the best in culture. Sign up for it here.

When Hurricane Helene hit western North Carolina in September 2024, it left behind it a trail of death and destruction—and a huge number of legal headaches. Residents struggled to understand the process of receiving help from FEMA, people were beset by scams and price gouging, and tenants whose homes had been destroyed or made uninhabitable puzzled over their rights.

Pisgah Legal Services, a nonprofit that provides civil legal aid in the region, had 120 staffers, and all of them were somehow affected, Jaclyn Kiger, Pisgah’s executive director, told me. Within about a week, though, Pisgah Legal had hosted three clinics; it ultimately helped thousands of Helene victims.

Civil legal services like these may not be well understood, but they serve an essential role in ensuring that everyone has access to justice. The Supreme Court’s landmark 1963 decision in Gideon v. Wainwright established that any criminal defendant has a right to legal counsel. But most legal issues are civil, not criminal. This includes any number of disputes between parties, but also issues such as evictions, scams targeting senior citizens, and domestic-violence protective orders. Recourse to courts in such cases can be lifesaving, but they are not free, and they are not easy for ordinary people to handle on their own.

Since 1981, every state has created programs to fund civil legal services with Interest on Lawyers’ Trust Accounts. It’s an elegant solution: Lawyers frequently hold money in accounts for clients, accruing interest that doesn’t truly belong to any party. IOLTA-funded programs use that interest to provide legal support without any cost to taxpayers. Kiger told me that Pisgah Legal received nearly $2 million in fiscal year 2025; overall, NC IOLTA provided clients roughly $12 million for civil legal services that year.

But in a budget passed this month, the Republican-dominated North Carolina General Assembly effectively cut this funding off. The budget permits $2 million in IOLTA funding in the coming fiscal year for civil legal aid, but that will sunset in the future. (Governor Josh Stein, a Democrat, signed the budget—the first that the state has managed to pass in more than a year—while criticizing elements of it, including the IOLTA cut, which he said “cruelly punishes low-income people who need access to justice.”) Most IOLTA money will be redirected to criminal legal services for indigent defendants, which the state is already constitutionally required to fund. Providers I’ve spoken with recently are crestfallen but also baffled. Although they all support funding for public defenders, they note that civil legal aid gets no other state money. “Hundreds of thousands of low-income people in the mountains are going to suffer,” Kiger said.

Civil legal services have historically been a bipartisan priority, popular among both parties and lawyers as well. Last year, a coalition of leaders of some of the state’s biggest law firms wrote a letter to the state supreme court backing IOLTA funding for civil legal services. “We believe a fair and functioning court system is not a partisan goal—it is a constitutional one,” they wrote. “By enabling litigants to participate effectively, IOLTA-funded legal aid strengthens both the efficiency and fairness of judicial proceedings.”

But some North Carolina Republicans have objected to the fact that some of the state’s biggest providers of civil legal aid also engage in progressive political advocacy or do legal work related to immigration or social-justice issues. The driving force behind the change in this month’s budget is state House Speaker Destin Hall, who is also an attorney in private practice. “IOLTA funds were shifted to ensure they’re not being utilized to bankroll progressive political pet projects like the NC Justice Center or provide government services to illegal immigrants. Instead, those funds will be used to provide legal services to underserved communities,” he said in a statement in June.

To that end, the general assembly placed political limits on a small portion of civil legal aid. The budget allows IOLTA money collected before July 2025 to be distributed, but only $2 million a year until the funds run out, and only to providers who don’t engage in political advocacy or take cases involving transgender health care or federal immigration law. This is less a way of removing politics from legal aid and more a way of instituting a different set of ideological priorities, and in any case, it applies only to this small and disappearing pot of money. The overarching goal appears to be not just going after progressive groups, but ensuring reductions in civil legal aid per se. This has led providers to speculate that the general assembly is less worried about “wokeness” and more interested in defending business interests. “I think it comes down to who we serve,” Kiger said. “We are serving the most vulnerable among us.”

When I contacted Hall’s office to try to understand the decision, a spokesperson asked me for detailed questions but never replied when I sent them. Ashley Campbell, who leads Legal Aid of North Carolina, the state’s largest civil-legal-aid firm, told me that she sought meetings with Hall but was rebuffed. In a statement, the executive director of the North Carolina State Bar lamented that “months of good-faith efforts” by the bar and NC IOLTA leaders had failed to sway the general assembly: “This loss of funding will prove devastating to the tens of thousands of low-income North Carolinians facing legal concerns that threaten their housing stability, income, access to health care and benefits, and safety from domestic violence and human trafficking.”

The defunding of IOLTA in North Carolina is a major event in the state, but it could have national implications. As I have been reporting for more than a decade now, many of the major currents in national politics have originated in the Old North State. The state has also been used as a proving ground, including for Donald Trump–style attacks on elections. Right now, the partisan warfare over civil legal aid in North Carolina is an outlier; Texas GOP legislators even strengthened IOLTA funding in 2023. But a failed Montana bill last year tried to weaken IOLTA, and Trump has proposed eliminating or drastically reducing funding for the Legal Services Corporation, a national civil-legal-aid group established by Congress. People I spoke with in North Carolina worry that the state budget could provide a template for other states as well. If that happens, access to justice could become just another luxury good.

Related:

Here are three new stories from The Atlantic:

Today’s News

  • President Trump said the nuclear agreement signed with Saudi Arabia yesterday is contingent on the kingdom normalizing relations with Israelby joining the Abraham Accords, a condition that had not been publicly disclosed when the deal was announced.
  • A member of Vice President Vance’s Secret Service detail is under administrative investigationand may face a criminal inquiry over allegations of compromising operations and information security. The Secret Service has not disclosed what information was allegedly compromised.
  • Global oil prices surpassed $100 a barrelfor the first time since May, after Houthi militants claimed to have struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.

Dispatches

  • Time-Travel Thursdays: Jake Lundberg explores what an office murder in 1841revealed about the American workplace.

Explore all of our newsletters here.

Evening Read

The New Chilling Effect

By Jacob Blane, Annabelle Garland and Sam Gross

If you’re looking for a reason to despair over the state of American education, there are many places you could start. You could, for example, worry about the all-consuming phones—or the gambling and TikTok addictions that come with them. You could worry about the

free fall in literacy, the decline in math fluency, the rise of AI, the insanity of college-obsessed parents, the sharp increase in truancy, the teen mental-health crisis, and on and on.

Yet something that deserves more attention is damage to free expression—not in the classroom but in the public sphere of high school: the realms of student newspapers, student government, and other aspects of everyday life. School is meant to prepare students for the real world, but too many institutions are training kids to be silent.

More From The Atlantic

Culture Break

Watch with everyone else. The Odyssey’s box-office success affirms the power of spectacle, David Sims writes.

Don’t just take the iPad away. In 2014, Daniel Willingham gave advice on how to trick your kids into reading all summer long.

PS

Katie Crutchfield, who records as Waxahatchee, is one of the best active singer-songwriters; her 2024 album, Tigers Blood, was one of my favorites of this decade. We probably can’t expect new music from her too soon, because she just had a baby, but two of her sometime collaborators have new records coming out that I’m hoping will tide me over during her absence. Brennan Wedl’s self-titled album (co-produced by Crutchfield) is out next month, and yesterday she released a rerecording of her excellent song “Two Dollar Pistol.” Jess Williamson, Crutchfield’s partner in the sublime one-off band Plains, is releasing A Mile South of Heaven in October, and the lead single, “Goodbye to All That,” is a promising tease.

— David

Rafaela Jinich contributed to this newsletter.
